responseSet.title: Closing Comments
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Closing Comments

George Engel M.D.

George Engel

George Engel  

The decision of the Editors to invite four prominent colleagues to write discussions of my remarks was a most felicitous one. I hope others will be prompted to express their views on this important issue and that the Journal will see fit to publish them.

The views expressed by the four discussants are quite divergent and, I suspect, fairly representative of current attitudes of analysts. The statements speak for themselves and I have no intention of elaborating any response. However, since others may wish to comment, I would like to clarify a few points where I believe my statements have been misinterpreted by one or another discussant.

Beres interprets the contrast I draw between the practitioner and the researcher as implying a dichotomy between clinical work and research. This is obviously a misinterpretation. Most analytic research is clinical, but not all clinical work is research. The contrast I meant is between the practitioner, whose major preoccupation is with psychoanalysis as a method of treatment, and the researcher, who deliberately takes advantage for scientific study of the circumstances afforded by a therapeutic analysis or who applies the psychoanalytic method or his psychoanalytic knowledge in nonanalytic situations (e.g., observation of children) toward the solution of scientific problems. I do not see how anyone could contend that the psychological style, personality characteristics, as well as the underlying ethos optimal for these two approaches are identical, though we might well wish they were.

Submitted June 22, 1967

228

[[RunningHead]]

Dreams Completely in Color

Richard Yazmajian M.D.

Richard Yazmajian

Richard V. Yazmajian, M.D.
167 East 67 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Richard Yazmajian  

SUMMARY

There is controversy in the literature as to whether dreams are essentially black-and-white phenomena to which color elements are sometimes added or are originally completely colored perceptions whose memory traces are subsequently "bleached" to varying degrees by repression and other mechanisms. The evidence in either direction is inconclusive and the issue should be considered to be a moot one.

A completely colored dream is one variety of dream about which there is little doubt. The occurrence of these dreams does not in any way settle the above issue since these dreams are very infrequent, and characteristically are unique in both form, coloration, and the quality of the subjective reaction of the dreamer during and after dreaming.

My findings have been that completely colored dreams give disguised, projected visual representation to body organs and parts which normally are never visualized, such as the distal aspect of the interior of the vagina, the testes, or the viscera. By reversal and projection, the invisible body part or organ is represented as being hypervisible and external through the use of pervasive hypercathected color elements. This technique is considered to be a carefully controlled, integrated, and artful utilization of color by the ego during dreaming—even where intense affects are associated with the manifest or latent content. This latter consideration is emphasized because of the stress in the literature on the relationship of affectivity and color in dreams as well as in other psychic phenomena.

Schreber and Flechsig—A Further Contribution to the "Kernel of Truth" in Schreber's Delusional System

William Niederland M.D.

William Niederland

William G. Niederland, M.D.
1143 Fifth Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

William Niederland  

SUMMARY

In accordance with Freud's suggestion in his Schreber analysis "to trace innumerable details [of the Denkwürdigkeiten] to their sources …," this study presents newly accumulated data and documented material pertaining to certain aspects of Schreber's delusional system. The place of these observations in the total picture of Schreber's psychopathology is briefly discussed. The careful evaluation of the material, also in connection with related

747

findings previously published (7), (8), (9), provides further clarification of various otherwise incomprehensible features of the Memoirs.

responseSet.title: Discussion
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

David Beres M.D.

David Beres

David Beres  

Engel is concerned with what he considers to be "the very inadequate state … of psychoanalytic research at the present time." So are we all, as indeed we should be in a science with so many unanswered questions. Certainly, we would also agree on the need to develop techniques for testing the clinical hypotheses which the psychoanalyst uses in his daily work, as well as the more abstract theories which comprise what is known as metapsychology. But we may not agree that this inadequate state of psychoanalytic research is indicative of a "failure" of some sort or that it is the result of the "history and present organization of psychoanalysis, that is, social and political." It may be, rather, that psychoanalytic research is itself in the process of development, influenced by intrinsic and extrinsic factors (as Engel also notes), and what appears as failure to some may by others be recognized as a slow growth that cannot be hurried. Even Engel's criticisms may, in this sense, be seen as part of the process of growth.

Engel begins by telling us that psychoanalysis is more than seventy-five years old, "a venerable age for many scientific disciplines," and this becomes an indictment of the failure of the "more than 1,300 members of the American Psychoanalytic Association" in research activities. Is seventy-five years so "venerable" an age for a scientific discipline? And what are the many other disciplines whose scientific achievements have reached such notable distinction within this period?

205

responseSet.title: Masturbation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masturbation

John Francis M.D.

John Francis

John Francis  

In his opening remarks, Manuel Furer said: "Masturbation is a ubiquitous phenomenon in our daily work, and as a clinical manifestation of infantile sexuality continues to raise far-reaching questions in reference to the drives and the ego and their collaboration in the act of masturbation, and in the masturbation fantasy. In recent times, there is increasing interest in the relationship of masturbation to the development of object relations." He also stated, "Freud described three periods of masturbation: first, the infantile period which raises questions about autoerotism, narcissism, and object relations; second, the oedipal period, which represents a culmination and unification of the partial drives under the primacy of the genital, into which is carried the complicated preoedipal history and from which individual sexuality receives a decisive organization by the resolution of the oedipus complex; and third, the last period of normal masturbation in puberty." He briefly drew attention to the growing body of differing opinions in the literature regarding the meaning of genital manipulation in the first two years, the fact that there is less controversy about the function of masturbation in the phallic-oedipal period, the effect of masturbation on structural differentiation in latency, and in puberty the part it plays as a normal sexual activity to facilitate object choice, although here it carries the danger of regression. The old question, "Is masturbation harmful?" continues to be a viable one.

John J. Francis presented a survey of the recent literature on masturbation, since the survey of the literature prior to 1961 given by Irwin Marcus at the panel on masturbation held at the meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1961.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Detroit, May 5, 1967. Chairman: Manuel Furer, M.D.

Anthropological and Historical Notes on the Female Sexual Role

Douglass Orr M.D.

Douglass W. Orr, M.D.
470 Nautilus Street, #312
La Jolla, Calif. 92037

Douglass Orr  

MARY JANE SHERFEY has suggested (20):

1. that precivilized woman enjoyed full sexual freedom and was often totally incapable of controlling her sexual drive;

2. that the transition from nomadic to urban cultures was very gradual because it took 5,000 years for the coercive suppression of woman's inordinate sexuality to take place;

3. that such suppression was a prerequisite to the dawn of every modern civilization;

4. that the subjugation of female sexuality carries with it the subjugation of woman's entire emotional and intellectual life; and

5. that modern writers mistakenly ignore the work of Bachofen (1) and others concerning the importance of matriarchal societies and the inordinate erotism of women.

What follows will not deny the subjugation of women. It will, however, cite anthropological, historical, and psychoanalytic data to indicate that these problems are not as simple as Sherfey suggests. If my statements are as sweeping as hers, they will at least provide alternatives.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Sylvan Keiser M.D.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser, M.D.
1100 Madison Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Sylvan Keiser  

Sherfey's undertaking is most admirable and necessary and one which could contribute to a deeper understanding of the complex problems of sexuality. In particular, she addresses herself to the problem of psychoanalytic theory of female sexuality and its relationship to observations culled from other scientific disciplines. Recent advances in embryology, anthropology, sociology, and sexual physiology are brought together here. From them she has developed revolutionary concepts about female sexuality and presented them with great vigor.

Specifically, it seems to be Sherfey's goal to effect changes in the psychoanalytic theories of female sexuality, and to discount or displace all previously accepted theories of female sexual development. It seems a rather difficult task to change a psychoanalytic theory without psychoanalytic observations, of which none are offered by the author. Although she does refer to some statements by some psychoanalysts whose opinions may lend some support to her contentions, she disregards the opinions of others. This study by Sherfey reflects her intensive and wide-ranging search for data in the various fields she regards as relevant for her theoretical formulations. Her scientific persistence and the novelty of her ideas and the possible contribution these might make to our deeper understanding of the complex mystery of sexuality are marred and, for me, almost invalidated by certain unscientific attitudes and a disregard of accepted scientific principles.

Division of Psychoanalytic Education, Downstate Medical Center, Brooklyn, New York.

Submitted March 23, 1967

449

[[RunningHead]]

Female Sexuality

Warren Barker M.D.

Warren Barker

Warren Barker  

In his introduction Marcel Heiman stated that the panel was set up to give psychoanalysts an opportunity to discuss two important publications on female sexuality, Human Sexual Response by W. H. Masters and V. E. Johnson,1 and a paper by Mary Jane Sherfey entitled "The Evolution and Nature of Female Sexuality in Relation to Psychoanalytic Theory."2 He expressed the hope that discussions at this panel meeting would result in the selection of a few topics within this complex subject matter for later study. Heiman pointed out that Masters and Johnson's description of the anatomy and physiology of the "sexual response cycle" is stripped of psychological matters to a degree neither attempted nor felt possible till now. This means that the description of the sexual response is incomplete, certainly for the human female. However, he went on to say that the exact observations of the physiological sexual process are as important as those having to do with salivation, mastication, glutition, and other body functions. He acknowledged that a great many eyebrows have been raised regarding the study of Masters and Johnson. Some objections are quite legitimate—for example, those to the effect that the presence of an experimenter modifies the results of the experiment. He deplored the fact that, on the other hand, many objections were not raised from the scientific point of view, but were made in a tone of moral indignation. Heiman said he could not, of course, predict the extent to which the findings of Masters and Johnson will be validated, but asserted emphatically that sex is a legitimate subject matter for scientific investigation on the anatomic-physiological level as well as on the psychological level. He expressed the belief that this was the first time that a panel had been organized to attempt to close the gap between psychoanalytic theory and psychoanalytic observations on the one hand and basic sciences such as anatomy

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Detroit, Michigan, May 6, 1967. Chairman: Marcel Heiman, M.D.

responseSet.title: Discussion
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer  

The criticisms of psychoanalysis brought forward by Engel carry us back nearly seventy-five years to Freud's early comments that his case histories read like short stories and seemed to lack the serious stamp of science. He attempted to console himself with the thought that his material gave him no choice and that the more traditional methods of research led nowhere in his work with patients.

Engel also recognizes that the material of the analytic therapist still limits his choice and is willing to grant that his methodology merits scientific approval for such fundamentals as empirical observations, data collection, and theory building; moreover, that his "notions" not only have been established "beyond any doubt" but have been verified by workers in other fields. If the trained research worker can assist the analyst in advancing from such an elementary "grade 1" on the scientific scale to a "grade 2," this could only earn his approval and gratitude.

There seems to be no preliminary need, however, to remind the analyst that his method of investigation is useful beyond the purposes of therapy (what, after all, were Freud's studies of dreams, wit, myths, etc., even if we go back nearly seventy-five years?). He has long been accustomed to correlate clinical data with findings in other fields: constructions as to a normal mental apparatus and norms of personality development and functioning constitute not only by-products but indispensable preconditions of analytic treatment.

211

responseSet.title: Discussion
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Elizabeth Zetzel M.D.

Elizabeth Zetzel

Elizabeth Zetzel  

Engel's paper raises many important issues pertinent to the current status of psychoanalysis in this country. We are becoming increasingly aware of dissatisfaction with our position in relation to general psychology, psychiatry, and research methodology. Our failure to elicit many applications for research training since the Committee on Training for Research was established has been a matter of increasing concern within the American Psychoanalytic Association. Engel's paper not only is relevant and timely, but will hopefully prove both stimulating and provocative.

Engel addresses himself primarily to the development of analytic investigators who, whatever their personal qualifications, should be using psychoanalytic technique as a major tool of research. It is therefore understandable, though regrettable, that he fails to distinguish clearly between psychoanalysis as a specific technique and the body of knowledge which forms the subject matter of psychoanalysis as a general developmental psychology. While this distinction is in many ways implicit in his argument, it is never made explicit. Although, in addition, he underlines some of the difficulties inherent in research in psychoanalysis, he nevertheless suggests that such problems should in essence be regarded as irrelevant and unimportant. "If the technique of analysis has to be modified, or if the analyst has to use a completely different method in order to gain certain kinds of information, so be it." This statement may well be applicable to research directed toward the general theory.

223

responseSet.title: Language and the Development of the Ego
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Language and the Development of the Ego

Henry Edelheit M.D.

Henry Edelheit

Henry Edelheit  

In his introduction Victor H. Rosen pointed out that psychoanalysis has long demonstrated the possibility of modifying psychic structure through the impact of verbal communication. Moreover, it has been shown that normal ego development depends, at least in part, upon an optimal level of verbal stimulation; and, conversely, that ego defects of any origin are reflected in disturbances of verbalization.

The Study Group for Linguistics and Psychoanalysis of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute was inaugurated in 1963 with the goal of acquiring from linguistics and related fields information, viewpoints, and methods that could be useful in psychoanalysis. Some of the researches of the group are reflected in papers prepared for the panel by Henry Edelheit and Samuel Atkin, and the organization of the panel was itself, in part, an outgrowth of the study group.

Following Ferdinand de Saussure, Rosen distinguished between language (la langue) and speech (la parole). Language is "a system of voluntarily produced symbols" (Sapir). It may also be described as a mnemic-representational organization within the ego. The term speech, Rosen recommended, should be reserved for the vocal-auditory aspects of verbal communication, and the term language code can serve to designate the shared verbal conventions of a language community. In addition to the strictly coded aspects of language (phonetic, syntactic, and semantic), auxiliary mechanisms such as volume, pitch, rate of delivery, and bodily movements play a part in communication. Rosen grouped these auxiliary mechanisms under the heading paralanguage. He preferred to attempt no compact definition of the ego, but suggested that the panel consider the minimal requirements for a structure that is able to store, encode, and decode language, in addition to performing the other well-known ego functions.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE TECHNIQUE AND PRACTICE OF PSYCHOANALYSIS: Volume I. By Ralph R. Greenson. New York: International Universities Press, 1967, xv + 452 pp., $12.50.

The extraordinary combination of comprehensiveness, scope, and lucid theoretical underpinning with the most luminous clinical illustrations contained in this book compels us to depart from the standard procedure of presenting brief synopses of content in this section without critical comment. There is little doubt that this promises to be the definitive work on psychoanalytic technique. There is not a single aspect of the subject which is not treated in the fullest and clearest detail, combining explicitness with uncompromising profundity. The author is never unaware of questions which may be left unanswered by his illustrations and elucidations. His pedagogic purpose is apparent throughout. One of the evidences is the appearance of additional reading lists at the end of each chapter. There is a fluidity and a flexibility throughout which preclude any possibility of finding in this book a set of rules, or of accusing the author of rigidity and "orthodoxy."

PROJECTIVE TESTING AND PSYCHOANALYSIS: Selected Papers. By Roy Schafer. New York: International Universities Press, 1967, viii + 229 pp., $5.50.

For the author, this collection of his papers is "a record of a series of explorations—of increasing complexity, ambiguity and difficulty—that have, I believe, heightened my own sensibility and may do the same for others." For Schafer, clinical "sensibility" is akin to artistic sensitivity and an absolutely essential attribute of the clinical psychologist whose approach is not merely confusedly eclectic or of the "common-sense" variety. By his own account, the author progresses in the series of papers embodied in this book toward increasingly "subtle aspects of subjective experience and the organization of personality" both regressive and integrative. The papers themselves, published between 1952 and 1960, reveal a clear and consistent application of psychoanalytic theory to Rorschach testing. They comprise such themes as content analysis, the assessment of personality change during psychotherapy, transference in the testee, schizophrenic reactions during testing, etc.

responseSet.title: Freud's Considerations of the Mental Processes—An Attempt at a Unifying Treatment
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Considerations of the Mental Processes—An Attempt at a Unifying Treatment

David Zern

David Zern

David Zern
Department of Psychology Vassar College
Poughkeepsie, N.Y.

David Zern  

SUMMARY

In essence, I have attempted to demonstrate in this paper how Freud has defended and argued for the crucial proposition that the mental processes comprise an integrated and coherent whole, despite the phenotypic disparity and arbitrariness in the observed behaviors. The discussion of the primary process emphasized its inability to delay gratification while searching for more efficient means of discharge. It is true that the dream (a significant example of primary process at work) releases tensions created in earlier

779

times. The point was made, however, that there was no element of deliberate selection involved in the dream. Rather, the direct discharge which so marks the primary process takes place when the stronger repressive influences that operate during the day "let down" at night.

Secondary process, on the other hand, is marked by three central characteristics missing in the primary process: (i) reality testing—in which the distinction between what is internal and external to the organism is determined; (ii) ability to delay—in which there is no tendency for immediate discharge, and where "experimental action" can be explored; and (iii) organizing ability—in which a "master plan" can be arrived at through a synthesis of key "tools" in the ego's arsenal. The contrast with the primary process is marked with respect to each characteristic.

The last main aspect of the paper dealt with a specific application of the earlier analysis of the mental processes. It was pointed out how defense mechanisms simply represent a somewhat inefficient operation of the mental processes to secure what always remains the underlying goal of the organism: the avoidance of unpleasure. It was pointed out how Freud recognized the inability of psychoanalysis to explain in an ultimate sense why defense mechanisms in general—and one specific defense mechanism in particular—are utilized, rather than more "normal" ego processes. But the point to emphasize in this consideration of mental processes in general is that an accurate representation of the world was seen by Freud to be of distinctly secondary importance to the organism's primary concern with avoiding unpleasure. A distorted perception-cognition which serves this end must always be distinctly preferable to accuracy accompanied by unpleasure.

I would not attempt to justify the position that the perspective Freud's work presented in this paper includes all the essentials of his principal achievements, the rest being mere commentary and/or application. Or, to be more accurate, at least I have not done this in the paper to an appreciable extent. The reader is certainly free to make such an inference, however. I would have no hesitation at all in arguing for the accuracy of Freud's own

780

evaluation of the place of this material in the corpus of his life's work and ambitions:

There is much in his work, and his implicit Weltanschauung, to recommend this statement as a valid one. I have tried to elucidate a small part of it.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Judith Kestenberg M.D.

Judith Kestenberg

Judith S. Kestenberg, M.D.
30 Soundview Lane
Sands Point, N.Y. 11050

Judith Kestenberg  

Sherfey's (12) impressive compilation of anatomy, embryology, and phylogeny focuses the attention of psychoanalysts once more on the biological basis of psychosexual behavior.

Her paper offers a stimulus to review the work of biologists on which she relies and to re-examine the foundations of psychoanalytic views on female sexuality. However, her attempts to reformulate psychoanalytic theory in conformity with new advances in biology fail because of a misinterpretation of certain biological data and a disregard of the two cornerstones of psychoanalysis—understanding of the unconscious and the insight that adult integration is an end result of progressive development.

The following brief critical remarks are based on clinical material that cannot be recapitulated here but is reported with other considerations fundamental to my comments (7)1

When we think that we have "penetrated through all the psychological strata and have reached bedrock," we look to biologists for facts that will explain "the great riddle of sex" (3, p. 252). And they look to us for a "reapplication of psychological and psychiatric theories based on what we now can show of physiologic and anatomic reaction" (8, p. 529). In trying to do so Sherfey suggests that: "Other than concepts based on innate bisexuality, the rigid dichotomy between masculine and feminine sexual behavior, and derivative concepts of the clitoral-vaginal transfer theory, psychoanalytic theory will remain" (12, p. 124).

Submitted March 7, 1967

1See pp. 457-520 in this issue.417

[[RunningHead]]

The Terms "Mother" and "Father" as a Defense Against Ingest

Martin Weich M.D.

Martin Weich

Martin J. Weich, M.D.
35 East 85 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Martin Weich  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to show the way in which the terms "mother" and "father"—and, to a lesser degree, other family epithets—play a symbolic part in maintaining and reinforcing the incest taboo in contemporary life. While I have focused on the effect of the incest taboo on family names, I do not mean to exclude the possibility of other modifying factors exerting an influence on the particular form of the name used in certain families. For example, a family which is formal and obsessionally removed from affects may encourage the terms "Mother" or "Mater," whereas another family may employ the more familiar forms "Mom" or "Ma" (see 21). A strict tyrannical father may insist on being called "Sir" or "S.J.," his initials used by business associates, etc. However, they are merely modifications and on the same usage level as the terms "mother" and "father."

Transference and Countertransference in Interracial Analyses

Judith Schachter M.D. and Hugh Butts M.D.

Hugh Butts

Judith S. Schachter, M.D.
Hugh F. Butts, M.D.
333 West 86 Street
New York, N.Y. 10024

Hugh Butts  

SUMMARY

Vicissitudes of transference and countertransference in analysis wherein analyst and patient differ racially permit these generalizations:

1. Obscuring or overestimating racial stereotypes by analyst or patient may induce a delay in the analysis.

2. Subculturally acceptable pathology may evoke overreactions, while racial stereotypes may be ignored.

3. Racial differences may catalyze analyses.

responseSet.title: A Further Attempt to Resolve the "Problem of Anxiety"
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Further Attempt to Resolve the "Problem of Anxiety"

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell, M.D.
456 North Carmelina Avenue
Los Angeles, Calif. 90049

Leo Rangell  

SUMMARY

A comprehensive psychoanalytic theory of anxiety, which applies to all instances of anxiety, combines elements of Freud's two historical theories of anxiety. The anxiety reaction is always set in motion by an existing traumatic state, either one which has invaded the ego involuntarily and from outside of its control (as in Freud's first theory); or one which has been brought about by the ego under its control in a minimal and experimental way (from Freud's second theory). This constitutes a twofold origin, in agreement with Freud's persistent idea. Both origins then converge in setting off a single common and unitary mechanism. The reaction is always a signal of the anticipation of danger (from the second theory). The danger is that of the traumatic state getting worse and out of control (first theory), or that the trauma which was sampled tentatively will come on in full force (second theory). The reaction always results automatically and involuntarily (as in the first theory), and is then used by the ego in an

401

active way (from the second). The reaction, which is experienced by the ego (second theory), has both id (first theory) and ego (second theory) components. The reaction itself is both a psychological (from second theory) and a somatic one (from first theory).

While neither Freud's first nor his second theory alone is complete in itself, the combination given, in my opinion, retains the valid and indispensable elements of each and can explain all the observable clinical phenomena. With this combined unitary explanatory theory the small but definite kink at the heart of psychoanalytic theory may be smoothed out, and Freud's "non liquet," I submit, can be changed to "liquet."

responseSet.title: The Masculine Envy of Woman's Procreative Function
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Masculine Envy of Woman's Procreative Function

Daniel Jaffe M.D.

Daniel Jaffe

Daniel S. Jaffe, M.D.
3741 Huntington Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20015

Daniel Jaffe  

SUMMARY

The man's envy of woman's procreative function has several sources:

1. The sequence of experiences which are universal and include:

a. The experiences of the oral phase which provide the first identifications with the mother and the association of her body and functions with the sense of internal fulfillment.

b. The contributions of the anal phase to the passive-receptive incorporativeness, which is the principal pregenital determinant of the feminine tendency in men.

c. Phallic cathexes which advance the equation of the mother's organs with penis and child, and the desire to possess all that the mother has inside of her as a defense against castration.

d. The castration threats of the oedipal phase which may strengthen passive feminine identification as a defensive tendency.

e. Difficulties in settling adolescent bisexual conflicts, which may prolong an identification with the mother.

f. Aggressive drives which, when increased by any factors at any genetic level, are of primary importance in producing conflict regarding tendencies and identifications.

2. The conflict between regression toward narcissism versus preservation of object cathexes, which is a residue in adult life of the conflicts of each antecedent developmental phase. The regressive tendency revives feminine identification (proceeds via homosexuality to the narcissism represented by the womb), and even in daily normal functioning presents a challenge to the phallic image of the self. The phallic self is reaffirmed through restitutive tendencies from whatever early experiences provided strength-giving identification with the intact father and the procreative mother.

3. The historical and cultural processes which emphasize matriarchal elements reinforce the masculine envy and the masculine need to usurp feminine organ modes and functions for reassurance of wholeness. Competing patriarchal influences in a culture provoke in the mothers conflicts over their identifications, from which ensue tensions affecting mutuality in mother-child relationships and reciprocal envy and identification conflicts in the males.

The patterns of development and regression, with evidences of conflict, may be recognized in analyses of men where resolutions are sought by sublimations based on identifications with the mother's good insides; or alternatively, symptom formation expresses the anxiety over such identifications, on account of projected incorporative and castrative threats.

The therapeutic process is one which includes the acceptance of regression, with new reintegrative (including constructive feminine) models offered, via the analyst's capacity to deal assimilatively with the patient's conflict products (interpretively, and with neither destructive incorporation nor castrative rejection). From

544

this, the conflict-free component of maternal identification becomes more readily available.

Some Obstacles to the Development of Research in Psychoanalysis

George Engel M.D.

George Engel

George L. Engel, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Strong Memorial Hospital
260 Crittenden Boulevard
Rochester, N.Y. 14620

George Engel  

SUMMARY

The aim of this paper is to stress the absolute necessity for psychoanalysis to develop a strong research cadre within its ranks. Some factors inherent in psychoanalysis per se and other factors relating to the present organization of psychoanalytic training are cited as responsible for the failure of such development to date. The present situation is regarded as critical and some suggestions are made for its improvement.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Theory of Affects
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Theory of Affects

L. Löfgren M.D.

L. Löfgren

L. Löfgren  

The discussion was opened by Charles Brenner, who emphasized how Freud was concerned with the importance of emotions from the very start of psychoanalysis, and how also a concern with affect and emotions permeates psychoanalytic theory, as evidenced by the very term "emotional illness" and by the emphasis on the importance of emotional versus theoretical insight. Freud linked the affect of pain or unpleasure to an increase of drive tension and the affect of pleasure to the discharge of drive tension. Thus, he greatly enlarged our concept of the scope of the role of affect in man's emotional life. A crucial contribution to the discussion of affect is Freud's Inhibitions, Symptoms, and Anxiety (1926), where he formulated his last anxiety theory. Since that time anxiety has been extensively discussed. The present panel, however, will discuss other affects as well.

From the last panel on affects, in 1952, there arose two major contributions to psychoanalytic theory, one Edith Jacobson's paper on "The Affects and Their Pleasure-Unpleasure Qualities in Relation to the Psychic Discharge Processes," and the other a paper by David Rapaport entitled "On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Affects." Brenner remarked that Rapaport expressly avoided reference to the subjective aspect of affects. He felt that this approach limits the value of Rapaport's contribution, as does Rapaport's emphasis on theory for theory's sake, as though a theory must be elegant to be useful. From Jacobson's paper Brenner quoted her concept that mounting drive tension and drive discharge are not incompatible with one another, at least in the complex mental functioning of the adult, whence the usefulness of considering the net result of the energic flux within the mind. Thus, three broad classes of affects or feelings emerge, according to Jacobson—feelings associated with tension, those associated with excitement, and those associated with relief of tension.

[[RunningHead]]

Types of Orgasm in Women—A Critical Review and Redefinition

Jules Glenn M.D. and Eugene Kaplan M.D.

Eugene Kaplan

Jules Glenn, M.D.
Eugene H. Kaplan, M.D.
21 Schenck Avenue (Dr. Jules Glenn)
Great Neck, New York 11021

Eugene Kaplan  

SUMMARY

1. The terms "vaginal orgasm" and "clitoral orgasm" are widely used but ill-defined.

2. Adequate definitions must take into account three factors: (a) the area stimulated (e.g., vaginally stimulated orgasm); (b) location of the orgastic experience (e.g., vaginally experienced orgasm); (c) the anatomical and physiological changes during orgasm.

3. Masters and Johnson found a basic pattern of anatomical and physiological changes common to all orgasms, always including vaginal contractions, independent of the area of stimulation, with marked variations in intensity and duration.

4. Correlations between observable physiological reaction and area stimulated are inconclusive. Masters and Johnson indicate that stimulation of the clitoris in masturbation often leads to more intense orgasms than those resulting from intercourse.

5. Masters and Johnson have demonstrated correlation between intensity of observed physiological response and intensity of the experience of orgasm.

6. We hypothesize a possible association of very intense physiological response in orgasm, intense grasping vaginal sensations, and oral-sadistic biting fantasies.

7. Additional investigation may demonstrate further correlations between the type of orgasm experienced and the physiological sequence observed.

8. The area in which the orgasm is experienced need not be the same as the area of stimulation.

9. There is a great variation in the location of the experienced orgasm. Sites other than the vagina and clitoris may be involved.

10. There is a great variation in the location of the area stimulated as well.

11. The type and location of orgasm experienced depend largely on the degree and distribution of cathexis of the mental representations of specific areas, the repression of sensations during orgasm, and the nature of the fantasies associated with the

orgasm. Both drive satisfactions and defenses against them influence the quality and location of the orgastic experience.

12. The location of the orgastic experience is a useful starting point for associations that will lead to the understanding of the latent unconscious meaning of the orgasm.

Psychoanalysis and Science

Charles Brenner M.D.

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner, M.D.
1040 Park Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Charles Brenner  

ONE OF THE PLEASANT perquisites of the presidency of the American Psychoanalytic Association is the opportunity to address the Association and its guests on a Sunday morning, as the Sunday morning lecturer. A Sunday morning lecture, by tradition, need not be as compact as the usual scientific paper. It allows the lecturer to present his views on a subject in a somewhat more personal way than is customary in a scientific presentation. What I have to say this morning will take advantage of this opportunity.

I have chosen to give my Sunday morning talk now, rather than six months or a year hence, as has become rather the custom in recent years, for a very personal reason. It was in 1942, twenty-six years ago, at a meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in Boston, that I first had the opportunity of addressing the Association in this very room. Partly because of the war, partly because the Association was so much smaller then, the meeting was very different in 1942 from what it is today. The entire scientific program occupied a day. It consisted of a few papers, not more than five, as best I remember, each of which was read and discussed in plenary session. My own role was that of scheduled discussant for one of the papers.

Twenty-six years ago, no sensible person would have dared to predict the tremendous growth of the Association that has in fact taken place.

Presidential address to the American Psychoanalytic Association, May 12, 1968.

Submitted July 2, 1968

[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Death and Neurosis

Max Stern M.D.

Max Stern

Max M. Stern, M.D.
960 Park Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Max Stern  

SUMMARY

This paper offers the thesis, based on clinical material, that in man adaptation to death is a necessary part of maturation and that deficiency in this adaptation is an integral factor in neurosis. It postulates that fear of death is fear of the repetition of mortal terror experienced in early ubiquitous biotraumatic situations defined as object loss or being abandoned by the mother, and that underlying mortal terror—the response in trauma—is primary depression, a state of sensorimotor retardation.

The paper adds to the classical psychoanalytic technique the working through of fear of death, which in many cases seems to be imperative for successful termination. We may formulate as the rationale of this technique that the neurotic uses his fixation to symbiotic dependency, which in infancy protected him against trauma, to ward off his fear of the inevitable final trauma. The amalgamation of obsolete anxieties of the past with the fear of the inevitable final trauma perpetuates the impact of early conflicts and reinforces the clinging to infantile dependency. In treatment

28

this is acted out in the clinging to transference. The working through of fear of death dissolves this amalgamation.

Cases are presented which were hitherto untractable and which, through this addendum, could be brought to termination. In these cases the interpretation of transference as protection against death brought to the fore a wealth of material usually neglected; it effected marked depression and regression to symbiotic wishes. This opened the way for the working through of the ambivalent symbiotic transference neurosis and led to a belated and more successful mastery of the once-deficient separation-individuation (38). This meant ego maturation in terms of establishment of identity, object relation, and sense of reality. It enabled the working through of the castration fears and the successful solution of the oedipal conflict.

The nature of fear of death, its relation to mortal terror and castration fear, are examined, as well as its impact on mental development and on neurosis.

George H. Pollock, M.D.
180 N. Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 60601

George Pollock  

IN A LETTER to his betrothed, Martha Bernays, dated April 28, 1885, Sigmund Freud wrote:

"I have just carried out one resolution which one group of people, as yet unborn and fated to misfortune, will feel acutely. Since you can't guess whom I mean I will tell you: they are my biographers. I have destroyed all my diaries of the past fourteen years, with letters, scientific notes and the manuscripts of my publications. Only family letters were spared… Let the biographers chafe; we won't make it too easy for them. Let each one of them believe he is right in his 'Conception of the Development of the Hero': even now I enjoy the thought of how they will go astray." [Jones comments:] While appreciating Freud's concluding chuckle in this interesting phantasy we nevertheless dare to hope that the last words may prove to have been exaggerated [16a].

The genetic approach, implemented by reconstructions of the past, is one of the fundamentals of psychoanalysis. The facts upon which such reconstructions are based are frequently hard to obtain, be it due to the forces of repression, or to realistic unavailability of crucial information.

From the Institute for Psychoanalysis, Chicago, Illinois and The University of Illinois, Department of Psychiatry, Chicago, Illinois.

[[RunningHead]]

Regulatory Mechanisms of the Perceptual Apparatus on Involuntary Physiological Actions

T. Dorpat M.D.

T. Dorpat

T. L. Dorpat, M.D.
2271 Northeast 51 Street
Seattle, Washington 98105

T. Dorpat  

SUMMARY

Psychophysiological regulatory mechanisms of the perceptual apparatus on involuntary physiological actions were first described and then explained with a psychoanalytic theory. The positive hallucination sensation regulatory mechanism involves the initiation of involuntary motor and secretory actions by hallucinatory sensations. The sensory hyperexcitability regulatory mechanisms involves the increased responsivity of a sensory system to peripheral stimuli and the consequent increased physiological response to such stimuli. The negative hallucinatory regulatory mechanism involves the inhibition of involuntary physiological responses to peripheral stimuli by means of negative hallucinations.

The data presented indicate a continuum of sensory responsivity with negative hallucinations on one end of the scale and positive hallucinations on the other. In between these extremes, different degrees of cathexis or countercathexis of a sensory system's memory traces provide a dynamic, variable threshold for the formation of sensations and consequent physiological activity.

The theory proposed to explain the positive hallucination mechanism is Freud's theory of the primary process. The only addition to the model of the primary process is the concept of the regulatory action of the hallucinatory sensations upon physiological actions.

The theory advanced to explain the negative hallucination mechanism is adapted from Freud's concepts of the stimulus barrier. In a situation where the sensory stimulation is unpleasant, the ego brings about a countercathexis against the sensory system's memory traces. This leads to suppression of sensation formation and consequently inhibition of physiological responses to peripheral stimuli.

[[RunningHead]]

Outside and Inside, Male and Female

Judith Kestenberg M.D.

Judith Kestenberg

Judith S. Kestenberg, M.D.
30 Soundview Lane
Sands Point, N.Y. 11050

Judith Kestenberg  

SUMMARY

Analyses of children and adults reveal the obstacles encountered in the development of the external orientation of men and inward orientation of women. Both sexes fear the destruction of their insides, but this more archaic form of the castration complex is typical for women.

There seem to be two "inner genital" stages in which anxiety-provoking inner genital sensations give rise to a reorganization of pregenital, inner genital, and outer genital component drives. The first "inner genital" stage ends with the subordination of all component drives to phallic dominance. The second "inner genital"

515

stage in prepuberty ends with the integration of all component drives, including the phallic, in the sex-specific genital primacy that begins with the onset of biological puberty. Adult genital organization cannot be achieved without the acceptance of the role of internal genital organs in coitus and reproduction.

The developmental trend toward externalization, common to both sexes, promotes adjustment to reality and facilitates the formation of psychic structures at the expense of sexualization of internal genital organs. However, this very trend obscures and retards the developmental steps that prepare the acceptance of the vagina as the primary erotogenic zone of the adult woman.

Throughout development the girl depends on external objects to alleviate her inner genital tensions. To completely overcome her fear of losing her "inside," woman depends on man to protect her against the danger of ego dissolution, represented in the loss of contact with reality at the peak of orgastic experience.

Analysis can free man from the fears and defenses which prevent him from accepting and carrying out his role of teacher and organizer of feminine sexuality. Analysis can free woman from the defensive masculinization that supports her externalization, denial, and repression of the vagina, and prevents the cathexis of vaginal excitation. Analysis can make woman teachable, but it cannot teach her. Woman's orgastic capacity develops to its full potential only when man can help her to achieve it.

[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Therese Benedek M.D.

Therese Benedek

Therese Benedek, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill, 60601

Therese Benedek  

New discoveries in embryology, physiology, and in the most recent branch of the behavioral sciences—ethology—seem to be lifting the veil from the enigma of female sexuality that has puzzled mankind from time immemorial. Freud in his last paper on the subject (14) frankly stated that psychoanalysis has not solved the "riddle of femininity" since "what constitutes masculinity or femininity is an unknown characteristic which anatomy cannot lay hold of" (p. 114); he added, "The explanation must no doubt come from elsewhere, and cannot come till we have learnt how in general the differentiation of living organisms into two sexes came about" (p. 116). Biology has not advanced far enough to give this ultimate answer, yet strides have been made in the direction of understanding the sexual behavior of both sexes. This new knowledge confronts psychoanalysis with the necessity of investigating its own concepts and by developing them further, to participate in capturing the "essence" of femininity, a task which will probably remain in the domain of psychology.

This is a problem where communication between scientific disciplines is essential for a solution. Such communication can and often does occur by a kind of "osmosis," an infiltration of ideas in such a way that the fertilization of one field by another remains unaccounted for. Yet the recognition of the "give-and-take" that occurs in the integration of scientific concepts is an important part of the clarification of issues which allied disciplines share. Without recognition of common problems and of mutually significant contributions, areas which need clarification might otherwise be disregarded.

Submitted January 4, 1967

424

Results of Psychoanalysis in Clinic Case Assignments

Fred Feldman M.D.

Fred Feldman

Fred Feldman, M.D.
450 North Bedford Drive
Beverly Hills, Calif.

Fred Feldman  

SUMMARY

The eleven-year history of the Clinic of the Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute has been reviewed and 99 cases accepted during that time have been evaluated. The results of analysis show that about two thirds (64 percent) of the cases were judged "good" or "very good" analytic experiences for the student analysts, and that the patients improved significantly. These results have been compared with those from other clinics, but only approximately. Some of the problems of analyzability and evaluation of results as they apply to clinics have been discussed. Our series appears to be the longest, consecutive, unselected series of analyzed cases reported.

In order to make statistics about "results" meaningful, a great deal more work must be done on the language of psychoanalysis. We can say at this point that a common, generally understood

299

mode of treatment is used by all student analysts, under careful supervision, and considerable control of the process is thus assured. Analysis is not an exact process, and a more precise language and formulation will enable us to record our proceedings and results more accurately.

Marcel Heiman

Marcel Heiman, M.D.
1148 Fifth Avenue

Marcel Heiman  

Sherfey's paper on "female sexuality" (3) encompasses a wide and diverse territory. I shall select for my comments that area of her paper which is of particular interest to me, i.e., her interpretations of recent findings in embryology.

I shall begin with a number of Sherfey's statements which will give us a good picture of her views: "The early embryo is not undifferentiated: 'it' is a female." Until the influence of the sex genes is brought to bear (fifth to sixth week of fetal life), "all embryos are morphologically females." Therefore, "we can no longer refer to the 'undifferentiated' or 'bisexual' phase of initial embryonic existence" (p. 43).

Since, according to Sherfey, the early embryo is a female, "Female development pursues a straight course," while "male development 'can be considered as a deviation from the basic female pattern'" (p. 49).

"Therefore [Sherfey states] the primacy of the embryonic female morphology forces us to reverse long-held concepts on the nature of sexual differentiation. Embryologically speaking, it is correct to say that the penis is an exaggerated clitoris, the scrotum is derived from the labia majora, the original libido is feminine, etc… For all mammals, modern embryology calls for an Adamout-of-Eve myth" (p. 50).

Attending Psychiatrist, Department of Psychiatry, The Mount Sinai Hospital, New York, and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, The Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

I am indebted to Marvin Fogel, M.D., Assistant Attending, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Mount Sinai Hospital and Assistant Professor, The Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

Submitted June 9, 1967

Panel Reports—Psychoanalytic Theory of the Instinctual Drives in Relation to Recent Developments

Hartvig Dahl M.D.

Hartvig Dahl

Hartvig Dahl  

In 1915 Freud wrote that in considering "mental life from a biological point of view, an 'instinct' appears to us as a concept on the frontier between the mental and the somatic, as the psychical representative of stimuli originating from within the organism and reaching the mind, as a measure of the demand made upon the mind for work in consequence of its connection with the body." Arthur Valenstein had proposed that the panel reconsider this concept of psychoanalytic drive from the vantage point of 1967 and from a knowledge of some developments in related fields, especially neurophysiology and experimental and physiological psychology.

Valenstein briefly sketched the history of the instinctual drive concept from the early pre-Project emphasis on childhood seduction and trauma and the associated idea of a repressed affect seeking discharge to the later recognition of the role of fantasies and unconscious wishes. The early view was what Rapaport called the environmentalist-empiricist view, and from it followed the idea that cure resulted from the abreaction of pent-up emotion. This view, however, proved to be too simple and its revision eventually led to an emphasis on intrapsychic determiners and the 1915 conception, which focused on the mental representation and emphasized the distinction between responses to inner and to outer stimuli. Valenstein said, "Freud saw it as crucial that stimuli from the external world could be avoided by muscular action whereas stimuli from the internal world which could not be readily escaped stood as the signs, the evidence of instinctual needs."

He described Freud's "bias toward a qualitative dualism of instinctual drives," that is, ego vs.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Implications of Recent Physiological Studies of Female Orgasm

Burness Moore M.D.

Burness Moore

Burness E. Moore, M.D.
152 East 73 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Burness Moore  

SUMMARY

This paper examines questions about the validity of the clitoral-vaginal transfer theory arising from anatomical and physiological evidence that vaginal orgasm, as distinct from clitoral, does not exist. Psychic rather than physical satisfaction seems to determine the preference of most women for vaginally induced orgasm, despite the fact that clitorally induced orgasms may be equally or more intense. This preference, biologically adaptive, is the result of an ego maturation influenced by self and object representations which contribute to a feminine identity based on woman's anatomy and species characteristics of lactation and intrauterine gestation of the fetus. Erogenous sensitization of the clitoris occurs during the nursing experience, accompanied by the psychic representation of the nipple-breast as part object. Loss of this part object contributes to the differentiation of the self from the object and together with other factors aids in the establishment of a complex of interrelated representations which form the basis for the well-known unconscious equation "body-nipple (breast)-phallus" and a continued cathexis of the clitoris. Although there is evidence that early vaginal sensations occur, the sensations are ill-defined, and invisibility of the female organs limits their perception and inclusion in psychic representation. The ego reacts passively to vaguely experienced sensations, and its passivity contributes to fantasy and poor reality testing in respect to the boundaries of the organs. Lack of ego control over the vaginal opening and inner organs also gives rise to anxiety and a need to repress awareness of the vagina, with a return to clitoral masturbation, defensive clitoral

585

hypercathexis, and penis envy. Pubertal changes re-establish erotism of the nipple itself with a reinforcement of clitoral cathexis until menstrual function, intromission of the penis, pregnancy and parturition complete the psychic representation of female structure and function. These vicissitudes of development contribute to greater cathexis of varying parts at different periods and determine whether sensation from the cryptic structures involved in orgasm are associated with the clitoris or the vagina.

Correlation of observations from other disciplines with those of psychoanalysis offers the possibility of expanding understanding of behavior only if a clear distinction is made between the psychic experiencing and objective observations of phenomena. If metapsychological factors are taken into account, instead of inconsistency, there is remarkable concurrence between current psychoanalytic theory and recent anatomical and physiological studies in respect to female orgasm.

It is suggested that cathexis of coitus and the achievement of intrapsychic change sufficient to improve significantly the object relationship be substituted for preconceptions about the desirable intensity and localization of orgastic experience as the criteria for improvement in cases of frigidity treated by psychoanalysis.

Studies in Audio-Recorded Psychoanalysis—I. General Considerations

Merton Gill M.D. , Justin Simon M.D. , Geraldine Fink M.D. , Noble Endicott M.D. and Irving Paul Ph.D.

Irving Paul

Merton M. Gill, M.D.
Justin Simon, M.D.
Brookdale Hospital Center
Linden Blvd. & Brookdale Plaza
Brooklyn, N.Y. 11212
Geraldine Fink, M.D.
Noble A. Endicott, M.D.
Irving H. Paul, Ph.D.

Irving Paul  

SUMMARY

This paper offers a critique of existing methods of research on psychoanalytic treatment and proposes audio-recording in order (i) to improve the data record, and (ii) to facilitate the separation of therapeutic from true research activities. Effects of research upon patient and analyst are considered and the objections to recording re-evaluated. We conclude that recorded research analysis needs to be done, that the crucial problems may lie in the therapist as much as, if not more than, in the patient, and we

242

believe that such an analysis can be successfully performed. If not, that demonstration in itself will constitute a valuable addition to our fund of information about psychoanalytic treatment and provide other leads for future psychoanalytic research.

[[RunningHead]]

"I Can't" Versus "He Won't"—Further Considerations of the Psychical Consequences of the Anatomic and Physiological Differences Between the Sexes

Marjorie Barnett M.D.

Marjorie Barnett

Marjorie C. Barnett, M.D.
2400 Lakeview
Chicago, Ill. 60614

Marjorie Barnett  

SUMMARY

The oedipal period is reconsidered from the point of view of the various anatomical and physiological differences between the sexes at that stage of development. The difference in orgastic capacity of oedipally aged boys and girls is considered. Hypotheses are drawn regarding the implications of these differences in the development and nature of object relations, the sense of reality, and later adult sexuality. An additional aspect of the resolution of the oedipal situation is postulated for girls. These hypotheses are used in an attempt to help understand the positive results obtained by Masters and Johnson in their sexual therapy program.

[[RunningHead]]

Denial and Ego Arrest

Sol Altschul M.D.

Sol Altschul

Sol Altschul, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 60601

Sol Altschul  

SUMMARY

1. Avoidance is the earliest and primary mechanism for dealing with unpleasant stimuli. Altering oneself, activity, and flight are added only later with the development of ego structure.

2. With the development of ego, the particular defensive

process of avoidance becomes organized into the defensive systems of the ego.

3. Upon this organization into specific defensive mechanisms, repression is directed toward the inside and denial toward the outside. Denial can, however, assist repression when the inner life approaches consciousness.

4. Denial may also serve and assist in the processes of adaptation and integration as in negation or temporary denial in mourning.

5. The differences between psychotic and neurotic, and adaptive versus defensive, depend not on the process involved but on the organization of that process in the ego, the interplay of structure and energy involvement.

6. Repression leads to fixation and impoverishment of drive function and object relationships.

7. Denial leads to arrests and impoverishment of ego function, variously called ego restrictions, ego distortions, or disturbances in ego development.

In conclusion, some of the controversy and confusion regarding the concept of denial have been presented. A possible resolution of these difficulties has been offered by viewing denial as a process of avoidance that becomes incorporated into the defensive-adaptive system of the organism upon structural development of the ego. In the process of comparison of repression and denial as an ego defense, a concept of arrest is offered with regard to the outcome of intrasystemic struggle that seems to clarify and distinguish it from fixation associated with repression and intersystemic struggle. Some terminological confusion still exists, particularly in regard to the multitude of phrases used to describe problems of ego development. I refer here to such terms as arrest, ego distortion, ego fixation, etc. However, I hope that some beginning clarification of this problem has been presented.

[[RunningHead]]

Female Sexuality—Introduction

Marcel Heiman M.D.

Marcel Heiman

Marcel Heiman, M.D.
1148 Fifth Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Marcel Heiman  

THIS PANEL ON "Female Sexuality" was set up to give psychoanalysts an opportunity to discuss two important publications on female sexuality. The first is Human Sexual Response, a book by Masters and Johnson, published in 1966, representing their studies over the past twelve years (2). The second is a paper by M. J. Sherfey (3) in the January, 1966 issue of this Journal.

No single person or group of people at any given time will find it possible to deal with all the aspects of female sexuality. Perhaps the discussions of this panel will result in the selection of a few topics within this complex subject matter for later study. It is my hope that this will be so.

I wish to make some comments of a general nature. Masters and Johnson's description of the anatomy and physiology of the "sexual response cycle" is stripped of psychological matters to a degree neither attempted nor felt possible until now. This might be an advantage or a disadvantage—but this is the way they did it.

I am sure that there will be good agreement that the description of sexual response from which the psychological elements have been left out, is incomplete—certainly for the human female. But that does not mean that exact observations of the physiological process are without value or do not contribute to our understanding of the total process of sexual response. This would be like saying that studies on salivation, mastication, and glutition are of value only if carried out in Soule's Restaurant.

Presented at the Panel on Female Sexuality, Annual Meeting of The American Psychoanalytic Association, Detroit, May 6, 1967.

Submitted June 17, 1967

[[RunningHead]]

On Manic-Depressive Psychosis—A Study of the Transition of States

Ping-Nie Pao M.D.

Ping-Nie Pao

Ping-Nie Pao, M.D.
Chestnut Lodge
500 W. Montgomery Avenue
Rockville, Maryland

Ping-Nie Pao  

SUMMARY

This study of manic-depressive psychosis is limited to the close examination of the circumstances in which the transition from one state or phase of the illness to another occurred. By way of clinical illustration this study concludes that both the mania and the incapacitating depression are regressive defenses selected by the ego to cope with aggression aroused by the experience of object loss, and that the success or failure in a flight to a new object in the face of loss played an important role in the choice of the defense. To keep within the limited goal, this study concerns itself neither with the structure, or the genesis of the structure, of the manic-depressive defenses, nor with the nature of the management of transference and countertransference problems.

[[RunningHead]]

The Process of Hypothesis Change in Three Early Psychoanalytic Concepts

Leo Sadow M.D. , John Gedo M.D. and Julian Miller M.D.

Julian Miller

Leo Sadow, M.D.
John E. Gedo, M.D.
Julian Miller, M.D.
George Pollock, M.D.
Melvin Sabshin, M.D.
Nathan Schlessinger, M.D.
30 No. Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill, 60602

Julian Miller  

SUMMARY

In an effort to study the mechanism whereby psychoanalytic propositions are changed, we have traced the history of the origins and evolution of three early working hypotheses. These are the hypnoid hypothesis, the seduction hypothesis, and the concept of the actual neurosis. Certain generalizations emerged from the detailed examination of the process by which Freud altered these concepts during the course of his long scientific career.

1. Freud attempted successfully to increase the generalizability of his hypotheses as new data were acquired. Thus, the seduction hypothesis evolved into the oedipus complex and a complex of observations and concepts around the idea of infantile sexuality; the concept of the actual neuroses became a metapsychologically subordinate aspect of the second anxiety theory; and the hypnoid hypothesis was dropped in favor of a much broader conflict-defense theory of psychoneurosis. The original statement of the hypothesis frequently was seen as a special case of the new, more broadly based hypothesis.

2. The alteration of psychoanalytic hypotheses in the direction of broader explanatory power is based on successful self-analytic work. This is most clearly demonstrated in our review of the development of the seduction hypothesis. Initially a projection, in large part, of his seduction fantasies onto patients, the seduction hypothesis evolved into the universal oedipus complex as Freud became aware of his own seductive wishes through his self-analysis.

The most important tentative conclusion to be drawn from our work is that a successful piece of self-analytic work is a prerequisite for useful hypothesis construction and alteration.

[[RunningHead]]

The Unconscious Fantasy of Infanticide Manifested in Resistance

Victor Calef M.D.

Victor Calef

Victor Calef, M.D.
2618 Jackson Street
San Francisco, Calif. 94115

Victor Calef  

SUMMARY

A woman whose basic character structure, driven by conscience, was typical of "those wrecked by success" came to analysis in a depression, suffering from compulsive indecision and doubt. It was possible to reconstruct that her illness was precipitated by the particular success that came with the purchase of a home, an unconscious symbolic reminder of fertility, past pregnancies, and past experiences with children, while it was also representative of financial and professional achievements far beyond even the dreams of her ambitious mother. Her successes had to be denied or destroyed because of their oedipal implications. The sequence of depression in women following the purchase of a home seems to be a psychiatric constellation which occurs more often than previously noted.

In the analysis the patient claimed stupidity, a lack of understanding, and an absence of involvement. She expressed sympathy and pity for the analyst as well as for herself while she attacked and condemned. Those attitudes expressed ambivalences and maintained isolations and undoings by which means she managed to destroy and rid from consciousness the work of the analysis. She nevertheless retained some therapeutic benefits from the unconscious aspects of the transference so long as they were isolated from the treatment. An unplanned pregnancy precipitated an

709

acute exacerbation of depression when her physician failed to grant her the therapeutic abortion she requested and finally had when it was justified by the depression. The rational wish to be rid of the pregnancy served the ego's unconscious defensive wish which, to be sure, arose out of instinctual aggression, but more importantly was determined by the effort to purge the oedipal victories and their attendant crimes. Thus the origins of the fantasy of infanticide were both instinctual and defensive; and eventually and typically the derivatives made themselves known first as therapeutic gains and then as resistances. The analytic work was experienced as an oedipal triumph and regressively as an oral gift which had to be eliminated (aborted) in the same way as the oedipal child had to be destroyed. The transference permitted the simultaneous existence of therapeutic gains and resistances and only after both were exposed was it possible for the patient to enter into the working-through and the final phases of analysis and the achievement of insight, demonstrating the diphasic character of the transference phenomena in analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of Culture in Psychoanalytic Theory and Practice

Stanley Jackson M.D.

Stanley Jackson

Stanley Jackson  

Warner Muensterberger introduced the panel by distinguishing between a theory of culture to which psychoanalysis can make significant contributions and culture as a complex of behavior patterns and beliefs that has an impact upon psychoanalytic work. For example, one may view culture as originating in and emerging from the need for defensive structures, or one may think of culture as an important factor in the choice of defenses. Our frequent references to "reality," our tendency to underline potential conflicts between internal stimuli and external necessities, point implicitly to a tension resulting from the ego's dependence on both an external setting and the demands rooted in the drives. Behavior is ultimately determined by the instinctual drives, but the environment coordinates their direction and provides the sociocultural conditions for adaptive measures. Environment in any particular instance reflects a complex of patterns from the larger cultural configuration, and it supplies "stimulus nutriment" (Rapaport) for defensive and controlling structures.

Portraying the "aspects of culture" in psychoanalysis in another sense, Muensterberger dealt with the psychoanalytic profession as a subculture. He related his feelings of obligation and satisfaction as chairman designate to a culturally defined order of values within the profession, reflected in roles assigned and in various statuses within the organization and their hierarchical ordering in terms of prestige. He commented upon the status sequence from applicant for training to member of the "American" as a gradual building up of accomplishments that reflect a profession's ideology and standards. In the anthropologist's terms, in this process one can note initiation rites, normative cultural values, reflections of kinship hierarchies, etc. And, Muensterberger added, a profession's system of values may embody traditions which are part of a reality system derived from organized religion or the state.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1967. Chairman: Warner Muensterberger, Ph.D.

[[RunningHead]]

Drives and Knowledge—A Speculation

Lawrence Friedman M.D.

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman, M.D.
50 East 72 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Lawrence Friedman  

SUMMARY

Piaget has suggested that the growth of thought is a process of compromise between stability and change. There is evidence that a poor relationship with human beings interferes with an infant's ability to learn about, and use, inanimate objects. Accordingly, it seems likely that a disturbance in human object relations may interfere with the stability which would allow the infant to comprehend new stimuli. This suggests that what normally provides stability in the face of the unfamiliar is a relationship between need states or drives and the environment. While abstract thought, according to Piaget, eventually develops logical procedures which bring uniformity and familiarity into novel situations, these logical procedures ignore that aspect of the world which is not abstract but concrete, not general but individual, not timeless but transient. In order to come to terms with a changing world of individuals even the mature mind requires the stability of an affective bond to the environment in addition to abstract concepts. Everyday "transferences" are thus essential to cognition, and aesthetic experience is a mode of adaptation.

[[RunningHead]]

Identification and Individuation

David Schecter M.D.

David Schecter

David E. Schecter, M.D.
120 East 89 Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

David Schecter  

SUMMARY

I have attempted to explore the historical development of the concept of identification and to offer some further developmental perspectives, especially as they are related to the process of individuation. Classically identification has been seen as a "monument to object relations" (26), and a "vicissitude of the id." I attempted to look at identifications as the building stones of a developing identity as well as a means by which the relationship to significant persons is kept psychically alive. The relatively autonomous roots of identification in the child's early tendencies to imitate, to seek out novelty, and to become effective in relation to his environment are considered.

When occurring under appropriate developmental conditions, identification can stimulate the process of individuation, including the transformation of passivity to activity, and of symbiotic relatedness to a self-reliant, yet reciprocal interdependence. At the same time, identification is a partial means through which these shifts or transformations in development come about.

Under conditions of traumatic separation, loss or frustration, identifications may be induced as defenses with varying degrees of adaptive or coping power. Five broad variables contributing to the adaptive outcome are considered clinically and theoretically:

a. the capacity of the ego to develop adequate intrapsychic structure;

b. the syntonic vs. traumatic nature of the loss or frustration—determined by the "dosage," timing, and quality of the experience;

c. the underlying affective conditions;

d. the quality of object relationship between identifier and his model;

e. the manner in which the identification becomes organized into the total personality, e.g., the selectivity of the identification, the resulting harmony or conflict with other aspects of the personality.

The phenomena of "pseudoidentification" are contrasted with those fulfilling the criteria of "authentic," "progressive" identifications. I have speculated that a temporary dedifferentiation of aspects of the self (as seen, for example, in various forms of fusion of self and object images, "I am you") may be a precondition for, or early stage in, the identification process. It is the interaction of such dedifferentiating fusion tendencies with the differentiating tendencies of identification ("I want to become like you only in this respect") that contributes to the eventual developmental outcome, whether this be a relatively individuated and integrated identity, or a fixation at the level of symbiotic union with the caricatured identity masks of pseudoidentification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein  

George Engel's passionate discourse touches a raw nerve of the body psychoanalytic, the state of crisis in psychoanalytic research, which is linked to and part of a wider crisis in psychoanalysis generally, but at the same time with very special historical and ecological determinants of its own. The larger dilemma of psychoanalysis in our society, the increasing criticism of our discipline both within the scientific-academic community and within the thoughtful and heretofore sympathetic intellectual community at large, has many sources—our collective failure to live up to the enthusiastic overselling of our therapeutic promise that ushered in our sweeping acceptance by educated America but a generation ago; the limited direct applicability of psychoanalysis to the vast problems of social and psychological dysfunction that mark our increasingly complex and troubled urban civilization; together with our aloofness, up to this point, for the most part, from the vigorous communal tackling of these major social, economic, and psychological problems, even in their specific mental health components, like the community mental health center movement, etc. Engel, in his all too brief exposition, hints at some of these issues of the larger context of crisis. In their different manifestations, they have become increasingly the object of thoughtful concern within our midst (1), (4). It would be a digression to enlarge on them here.

Chief, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Zion Hospital, San Francisco.

215

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mary Jane Sherfey: "The Evolution and Nature of Female Sexuality in Relation to Psychoanalytic Theory"

This article was published in Volume 14 of this Journal. In it Sherfey proposed that data pointing to embryonic female primacy and the contributions of Masters and Johnson on female sexuality would require "amendation of psychoanalytic theory."

The interest aroused by this article culminated in a panel discussion devoted to an evaluation of Sherfey's contribution and those of Masters and Johnson. In addition, the Editors decided to invite a number of analysts to contribute written discussions of Sherfey's paper. At the same time, several papers on female sexuality became available. The publication of all these contributions in this issue of the Journal reflects the great current interest in psychoanalytic concepts of female sexuality.

405
